preparation_method: maceration of vanilla beans in ethanol-water solution

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of vanilla extract is 1 teaspoon (approximately 4.2 g), used as a flavouring agent in baked goods, desserts, and beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Vanilla extract — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 2262075 / NDB 02050)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for vanilla extract (NDB 02050) used as primary nutrient anchor. Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB source data provided in input record. HPB values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy. USDA used as primary approved source; HPB values preserved where consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from sucrose/sugar-based liquid extract analogy; vanilla extract is used in trace culinary quantities (approx. 4.2 g per serving) making glycemic load negligible
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate basis: sucrose GI = 65)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vanilla extract in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Vanilla extract's carbohydrate content (12.65 g/100 g) is predominantly sucrose-derived sugars. GI estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with sucrose (GI 65, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per 1-teaspoon serving (4.2 g) = (65 × 0.53 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.34, rounded to 0.3 — clinically negligible at typical culinary doses.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (9 mg/100 g); used only in trace culinary quantities of 1–5 ml per recipe
hypertension.guidance: Vanilla extract contributes negligible sodium at typical culinary doses (1 teaspoon ≈ 0.4 mg sodium) and poses no meaningful hypertension risk; assess the overall dish's sodium content rather than the extract in isolation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content 12.65 g/100 g is irrelevant at typical culinary doses of 1–5 ml per recipe; glycemic load per teaspoon is approximately 0.3
type2_diabetes.guidance: At a standard 1-teaspoon (4.2 g) serving, vanilla extract contributes approximately 0.53 g of sugar and a glycemic load of 0.3, which is clinically negligible; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should assess the total sugar content of the recipe rather than the extract alone, and avoid substituting with sweetened vanilla syrups or pastes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.06 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and trace saturated fat; vanillin polyphenols have demonstrated antioxidant properties in vitro
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vanilla extract contains virtually no fat, saturated fat, or cholesterol, and its vanillin content provides mild antioxidant activity; it poses no cardiovascular risk at typical culinary doses and may be freely used in heart-healthy cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vanillin and polyphenolic compounds with mild antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; no pro-inflammatory saturated fat or trans fat at culinary doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vanilla extract used in trace culinary amounts contributes no meaningful pro-inflammatory components; assess the overall dish for pro-inflammatory ingredients such as sugar and saturated fat rather than the extract alone.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vanilla extract is a dark brown, thin liquid flavouring agent produced by macerating cured vanilla beans (Vanilla planifolia) in an ethanol-water solution, typically at a ratio of 35–40% alcohol by volume for pure extracts. It is one of the most widely used flavouring ingredients in global baking and confectionery, imparting an intense, sweet, floral vanilla aroma and taste. The extract is used in very small culinary quantities — typically 1 to 5 ml (approximately 1 teaspoon) per recipe — meaning its per-100 g nutrient values, while measurable, are clinically irrelevant at actual consumption doses. Per 100 g, vanilla extract provides 288 kcal, 12.65 g carbohydrate (predominantly sugars), 0.06 g protein, 0.06 g fat, and 9 mg sodium. Its glycemic load per teaspoon serving is approximately 0.3, which is negligible. Vanilla extract contains vanillin and other polyphenolic compounds that exhibit antioxidant properties in vitro. It is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when used in standard culinary quantities. It is plant-based, gluten-free, and dairy-free, though it contains alcohol and is therefore not universally halal-certified. It should not be confused with vanilla essence (an artificial flavouring) or sweetened vanilla pastes and syrups, which have substantially higher sugar content. Common applications include cakes, cookies, custards, ice cream, puddings, and beverages.
common_user_queries: Is vanilla extract safe for diabetics?
How many calories in vanilla extract?
What is the difference between vanilla extract and vanilla essence?
Can I use vanilla extract if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of vanilla extract?
How much sugar is in vanilla extract?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02050), an approved primary source, and cross-confirmed with Singapore HPB input data. Values are consistent across both sources. GI is an analogous estimate from sucrose (University of Sydney GI Database) with full documentation, as no direct GI measurement exists for vanilla extract.
notes: Protein_g (0.06) and fat_g (0.06) are very low but non-zero trace values confirmed by USDA SR Legacy and HPB; these are not zero-macro cases requiring special justification. Saturated_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy (reported as 0.0 g/100 g). Trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed absent per USDA SR Legacy. Dietary_fibre_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and HPB. Cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and HPB — vanilla extract is a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, and all vitamin/mineral micronutrients beyond potassium, calcium, and iron set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, moisture, and full micronutrient panel not reported in USDA SR Legacy NDB 02050 or Singapore HPB database for this entry as of 2026. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 65, medium) based on sucrose from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for vanilla extract exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per 1-teaspoon (4.2 g) serving = 0.3, clinically negligible. Halal status marked false due to ethanol content in pure vanilla extract; alcohol-free vanilla flavouring alternatives exist. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT 227512008 are defensible matches; FoodEx2 A0FKP is the closest available code for vanilla extract in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
